segments.s370-g145: نجنبید is a negative past (3rd-person plural) form meaning “did not move/stir.” The phrase ز ایشان کس (here written together as زیشان کس) literally = “anyone from them”; with the verb negated it yields “not one of them.” از جای خویش is a possessive phrase “from his own place,” where جای = “place” and خوی + ش supplies the reflexive/possessive “own/his,” so the whole segment says “not one of them moved from his place.”
segments.s370-g146: ز (poetic variant of از) marks origin/agency: “from the army.” نیاورد is the negative past tense “did not bring” used idiomatically with پای پیش (“foot forward”) — Persian idiom for advancing a step — so نیاورد کس پای پیش = “no one put a foot forward.” The verb precedes کس (indefinite/negative ‘no one’) because of poetic inversion; پیش here functions adverbially as “forward/in front.”
segments.s370-g113: This is a nominal phrase in apposition: سر (head) is the head noun, followed by جادوان جهان which identifies the owner ('the world's sorcerer(s)/enchanter(s)'), and the epithet بیدرفش ('without a banner') qualifies that noun. Word order is noun + modifiers (typical in Persian poetry), so read as 'the head of the world's sorcerer, the bannerless.'
segments.s370-g114: This clause contains the verbal action. مر is the first‑person object 'me' placed before the verb for emphasis, او را is the third‑person object with the explicit object marker را, and بیفگند is an archaic/polite past 3rd‑person form of افکندن (to throw) meaning 'he threw'. The coordinating و links the two verbs: بیفگند ('threw to me') و برد (here 'took' / 'carried off') with آن درفش ('that standard') as the object of برد.
segments.s370-g115: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی functions as 'news/information' and is the subject of رسید ('reached/arrived'). کشتن او is a verbal noun phrase 'the killing of him' (کشتن + object او), so the literal order is 'When the news of his killing reached (someone)...'.
segments.s370-g116: به marks the indirect object 'to' or 'for' and introduces شاه جهانجوی ('the king, world‑seeking' — an epithet after شاه). The coordinating و links the next clause: مرگش ('his death') is the object of بدید, where بدید is the simple past 'saw' (3rd singular) meaning 'he saw his death / that he was dead.'
segments.s370-g117: همه جامه is 'all the garments' (subject). تا پای is a locative/extent phrase 'down to the feet' (تا ... پای = 'as far as the feet'). بدرید is the past plural 'they tore', and پاک here functions adverbially 'completely/utterly' following the verb.
segments.s370-g118: بران is an older/poetic contraction meaning 'upon him/thereon'. خسروی ('royal') qualifies the person (the king), تاج is 'crown' and پاشید is the past 'they scattered/sprinkled'. خاک ('dust') is the thing scattered and follows the verb in object position. Literal order: 'upon him the royal crown they sprinkled dust.'
segments.s370-g119: همی گفت is a habitual or iterative past construction meaning 'kept saying' or 'continued to say' (همی + past verb). گشتاسپ is the speaker's name in the nominative position, and کای شهریار is a vocative 'O king' (کای = 'O', شهریار = 'king'), addressing the monarch directly.
segments.s370-g120: چراغ دلت is a possessive genitive phrase 'the lamp/light of your heart' where دل + second‑person suffix ت marks 'your heart'. را is the object marker attached to that phrase. بکشتند is the past 3rd plural 'they killed/they have slain' (the subject is those who committed the deed), and زار is a lamenting particle/adverb ('alas' / 'woefully') adding an emotive tone.
segments.s370-g33: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ش 'his/its' (possessive), so 'his army'. ندادند is the past 3rd‑person plural negative of دادن 'they did not give'. پاسوخ is the object 'answer' and باز is an adverb here meaning 'back/again' (i.e. 'returned no answer'); the verb follows the possessive NP in a slightly inverted, poetic order.
segments.s370-g34: بترسیده is a past participial/predicative form meaning 'became frightened' (result/ state). بد functions adverbially here ('badly' / 'severely') modifying the verb. لشکر سرفراز is a noun + postpositive epithet 'the proud/victorious army'; the copula 'شد' is omitted in this line, so the sense is 'the proud army became badly frightened.'
segments.s370-g35: چو at the start acts as a temporal conjunction 'when'. شیر اندرافتاد is a simile/verb phrase 'fell upon (them) like a lion' — اندرافتاد is an older past form 'fell upon/attacked'. The second چون introduces the second simile 'like an elephant', and مست is a descriptive adjective 'intoxicated/mad' modifying پیل to create a fierce-image comparison.
segments.s370-g36: همی is an archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or repeated action (common in epic Persian). کشت is the past-stem meaning 'he killed/was killing'; زیشان ('ز' + ایشان) means 'from/of them' (i.e. 'them' as direct objects). The second همی again marks ongoing action and کرد is the causal/auxiliary 'made/did', with پست as the resulting adjective 'low/despised' — together 'kept killing them and made them low.'
segments.s370-g37: همی again marks repeated/continuous action. کوفتشان is the verb کوفت + enclitic شان 'them' (they/ him/it struck them or he kept striking them, depending on subject established earlier). هر سوی 'on every side' (هر = every, سوی = side/toward). زیر پای is a locative phrase 'under the feet/underfoot' describing where the blows land.
segments.s370-g38: سپهدار ایران is a genitive construction 'the commander of Iran' (سپهدار = commander/title; ایران = Iran). فرخنده رای is an epithet postposed to the title: فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' modifies رای 'counsel/plan', producing 'of auspicious counsel' — a praise phrase attached to the commander.
segments.s370-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ارجاسپ دید is straightforward past 'Arjasp saw'. آن‌چنان is an intensifier 'so/so much', and خیره شد is a compound predicate 'became astonished/stunned' (خیره 'staring/astonished' + شد 'became'), giving 'he became so astonished.'
segments.s370-g40: که introduces the result clause 'that...'. روز سپیدش is a noun phrase 'his bright/white day' (سپیدش = سپید + enclitic ش 'his'; here سپید = 'bright/pure'). شب تیره شد is 'became a dark night' (تیره = dark); the verb شد 'became' completes the result: 'that his bright day turned into a dark night.'
segments.s370-g57: سه بار = 'three times' (numeral + measure word). این سخن را is a direct-object phrase marked by را (definite object marker) so 'this speech/these words' is the object. بریشان is بر + ایشان (the preposition/element بر meaning 'toward/against' with the 3rd-person plural clitic), and براند is an older/perf. form of راندن 'to drive/direct' (here used transitively: 'drive/direct/say to'); together it yields 'he directed/put those words toward them (i.e. said them to them) three times.'
segments.s370-g58: چو here = 'when'. پاسخ نیامدش: پاسخ 'answer' + نیامد (did not come) with the enclitic -ش = 'to him/for him' → 'no answer came to him.' خامش بماند is verbless-in-appearance sequence where خامش 'silent' functions predicatively and بماند (ب-ماند, classical past/preterite or stative) = 'he remained/kept' → 'he remained silent.'
segments.s370-g59: بیامد = 'came' (simple past). پس here = 'then'/'after that' (connector). آن = the demonstrative 'that.' بیدرفش is a compound adjective (بِی‑درفش) meaning 'without a banner/standard' (بِی- = without, درفش = banner). سترگ is an epithet attached to that noun phrase (an archaic adjective meaning 'large/lofty' or 'broad-eyed' in poetry) qualifying the same referent: 'that great/bannerless one.'
segments.s370-g60: A string of adjectival epithets joined by و (and): پلید 'vile/base', بد 'bad/evil', جادوی 'magical' (adjective form), و پیر گرگ 'and an old wolf' (پیر 'old' modifying گرگ 'wolf'). This piled-up descriptive list is typical of epic diction.
segments.s370-g61: به ارچاسپ گفت = 'he said to Arjasp' (به marks the indirect object/recipient). The following ای بلند آفتاب is a vocative address: ای 'O!', بلند 'lofty' (epithet), آفتاب 'sun' used metaphorically as a form of address.
segments.s370-g62: به زور و به تن = two parallel adverbial phrases introduced by به (here functioning adverbially: 'in/with regard to'): به زور 'in force/strength' and به تن 'in body/physically'. همچو = 'like' + افراسیاب (name) gives the simile 'like Afrasiab.' The phrase modifies the vocative, attributing strength and bodily likeness.
segments.s370-g63: به پیش تو آوردم = 'I brought before you' (به پیش = 'before/in the presence of', تو = 'you', آوردم = 1st-person past 'I brought'). این جان خویش combines این جان 'this life/this soul' with خویش + -ش (خویش is part of the possessive construction and -ش is the 3rd-person possessive clitic here functioning as 'my/his/her' in classical usage), yielding 'this my life' (i.e. 'this life of mine') placed before 'you.'
segments.s370-g64: سپر کردم = 'I made (it) a shield' (سپر 'shield' as object, کردم = I made/did). این جان شیرینت پیش continues the object phrase: این جان 'this life' + شیرینت = شیرین + -ت ('sweet' + 2nd-person possessive clitic) so literally 'this sweet (one) of yours' or 'this your sweet life'; پیش again 'before/ in front of.' The whole clause means 'I made this sweet life into a shield before you' (i.e. offered/used as a shield).
segments.s370-g121: ز پس is a poetic temporal phrase meaning “after that/thereupon.” گفت is the past verb “said” placed before the subject for poetic inversion; داننده (the knower/expert) is the agent and جاماسپ را is the indirect object with the accusative/topic marker را (here functioning like “to Jamasp”).
segments.s370-g122: چه گویم کنون is a direct question: چه 'what' + گویم, first‑person present/subjunctive form meaning “shall I say / should I say” (poetic use of the present tense), and کنون “now.” شاه لهراسپ را is the object/topic “King Lohrasp” marked by را. The whole segment is a single question addressed to Jamasp.
segments.s370-g123: چگونه is the question word “how.” فرستم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive “shall I send / I should send” (poetic use), and فرسته is the noun “envoy/messenger” as the direct object; بدر is a proper name (Badr) giving the destination/recipient. Word order is inverted for verse.
segments.s370-g124: چه گویم is again “what shall I say.” The segment then reads بدان پیر گشته پدر: here ب + دان in the manuscript is the poetic split of بدان (به + آن) meaning “to/unto that”; پیر گشته is a participial phrase “become old / aged” (گشته = perfect/participial ‘become’), and پدر “father” (here ‘that aged father’).
segments.s370-g125: This is two short exclamatory questions: چه گویم “what shall I say?” and چه کردم “what have I done?” نگار ترا is the direct object “my beloved / my darling” with the 2nd‑person suffix ت (your) on نگار and را as object marker. The verb کردم is past tense first person “I did / I have done.”
segments.s370-g126: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “that/who.” برد here is the past of بردن “took / carried off” (the provided lemma senses did not list this verbal sense, so mapped -1). آن is the demonstrative “that”; نبرده is a negative participle/past form “had not taken”; سوار is “rider/horseman”; ت is the 2nd‑person possessive suffix “your”; and را is the object marker. Together: “who took you, whom no rider had taken before.”
segments.s370-g127: دریغ is an interjection “alas/pity.” آن گو شاهزاده دریغ is a compact clause: آن گو (that‑saying) modifies شاهزاده “the prince,” and the repeated دریغ is the prince’s lament — poetically the speaker is identified and then utters “dariġ” again.
segments.s370-g128: چو is the conjunction “when.” تابنده ماه is a poetic epithet “the shining/radiant moon” (تابنده = shining, ماه = moon/beautiful one). اندرون شد is “went/entered within” or “became inside” (شد as past/become). به here marks the instrument or relation and is used like “by/with,” and میغ means “blade/point”; the clause describes the radiant moon being struck/affected by the blade. Word order and uses are poetic and somewhat compressed.
segments.s370-g81: بینداخت is a past tense verb (3rd sg.) meaning 'threw/cast' and precedes its object here (typical Classical/poetic word order). ژوپین is the direct object 'gambeson / padded jacket' and زهرابدار is a following adjective/compound (zahrābdār) modifying ژوپین meaning 'poison‑laden' (adjectives commonly follow the noun).
segments.s370-g82: ز + پنهان is a prepositional phrase 'from (the) concealment / out of hiding' (ز is the poetic preposition). بران is the contracted compound 'بر آن' = 'upon/at that' referring to the following phrase; شاهزاده سوار is a noun phrase 'princely rider' (شاهزاده 'prince' + سوار 'mounted/rider'). The whole segment describes the manner/place of the action: from hiding he charged at that mounted prince.
segments.s370-g83: گذاره شد is an archaic/past passive construction where شد functions as the auxiliary 'was' and گذاره (here) conveys 'was put/was cast/was set aside' (passive meaning). از خسروی is a genitive phrase 'from/of the king' (خسروی = royal, of the king) and جوشنش = جوشن + ـش 'his cuirass/coat of mail' (possessive suffix). Together: 'from the king his cuirass was cast aside / was removed.'
segments.s370-g84: به خون غرقه شد is a result clause: شد (auxiliary) makes غرقه 'drenched/immersed' into a passive 'was drenched'; به here indicates the state 'in/into' (drenched in). شهریاری تنش = شهریاری (kingly/royal) modifying تن + ـش 'his body' — 'his kingly body.' The sequence states the bodily result: 'his royal body was drenched in blood.'
segments.s370-g85: ز باره در افتاد is an idiomatic/periphrastic phrase: ز (poetic 'from') + باره ('occasion/turn/encounter') + در افتاد (افتاد 'fell/occurred' with preposition در) produces 'fell into the encounter' or 'fell at that turn' — here meaning 'then he fell in the clash'. پس 'then' marks sequence, and شهریار is the noun 'the king' serving as the logical subject/patient of افتاد.
segments.s370-g86: دریغ is an exclamatory/prefatory noun 'pity/alas'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. نکو is a postpositive adjective 'good/fine/noble' modifying شاهزاده, and سوار completes the noun phrase as 'princely rider' — the whole short phrase expresses lament: 'Alas for that fine prince on horseback.'
segments.s370-g87: فرود آمد is a two‑word past tense verb meaning 'alighted / came down' (آمد as the main verb with فرود indicating descent). آن is the demonstrative 'that', and بیدرفش پلید is a descriptive compound: بیدرفش 'without banner' (bī‑derafsh) followed by پلید 'vile' — together describing the person who alighted.
segments.s370-g88: سلیحش = سلیح (weapon/armament) + ـش (his) is the direct object 'his weapons'. همه 'all' quantifies them, پاک here functions adverbially 'cleanly / completely', and بیرون کشید is a compound verb 'drew out / pulled out' (past 3rd sg.). The clause means 'he drew out all his weapons completely.'
segments.s370-g41: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'again' (دگر + باره = 'another/again occasion') placed before the verb. گفت is the simple past 'he said' (third person). ای بزرگان من is a vocative: ای marks direct address, بزرگان is the plural vocative 'great ones', and من is a possessive sense ('my') attached after the addressed noun (poetic order).
segments.s370-g42: This is an appositive/continuation of the address. تگینان is a plural title (Tegins, nobles/commanders) used vocatively; لشکر گزینان من literally 'army (لشکر) — the chosen (گزینان) of mine', where گزینان functions as a postpositive adjective/noun 'chosen' and من marks possession 'my'. The whole phrase expands who he is addressing.
segments.s370-g43: ببینید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'look/see' (the -ید ending). خویشان و پیوستگان is a noun phrase in direct address: 'kinsmen (relatives) and پیوستگان (followers/attendants)'. The coordination with و simply links the two groups being addressed.
segments.s370-g44: Again begins with the imperative ببینید 'see/behold'. نالیدن is the noun/infinitive 'lamenting' (the act of wailing) and خستگان is the genitival noun 'the weary' (literally 'the lamenting of the weary'); Persian often places the possessed/agent after the verbal-noun, here creating 'نالیدن خستگان' = 'the laments of the weary.'
segments.s370-g45: ازان is the contracted form of از آن 'from that/of that', introducing source. زخم 'wound' and آن پهلو 'that flank' are in apposition/genitive relation (poetic ordering), so the phrase means 'from that wound of that flank'. آتشی is an adjective/noun 'a fire/fiery thing' describing what issues from the wound.
segments.s370-g46: که is a relative pronoun 'which/that' linking back to آتشی. سامیش is a possessive form referring to Sam (i.e. 'Sam's'), and گرزست is a predicative form 'is a mace' (گرز + -ست 'is'); the clause says the fire is like 'Sam's mace'. Then و تیر آرشی adds 'and (is) the arrow of Arash' — a parallel identification. This is a descriptive relative clause identifying the destructive force as comparable to famous weapons/men.
segments.s370-g47: که introduces a clause referring back to the fire described earlier. گفتی is the 2nd-person-past 'you said' (reporting what was spoken). بسوزد is a subjunctive/optative or reported-future sense 'would burn/would set on fire' with همی as an emphatic particle 'indeed/just', and لشکرم 'my army' is the object (لشکر + possessive -م). Together: 'which you said would burn my army.'
segments.s370-g48: کنون is the adverb 'now'. برفروزد is a present/future-style verb 'will kindle/set ablaze' (بر-فروز + -د) describing the fire's present effect. همی again is emphatic 'indeed/now', and کشورم 'my country/realm' is the object with possessive -م. The clause states the immediate consequence: now it will burn my realm.
segments.s370-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. شد is a past verb 'became/turned' (here marking the event). جادوی is the noun 'sorcery/magic' followed by two postpositive adjectives زشت and ناباکدار ('ugly/vile' and 'shameless'), so the whole phrase means 'the ugly, shameless sorcery' and functions as the subject of شد.
segments.s370-g74: سوی marks direction 'toward'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. خردمند is an adjective 'wise' modifying the head noun سوار ('rider/horseman'). گرد here has the sense 'around/encircling' (poetic positioning), so سوی آن خردمند گرد سوار = 'toward that wise rider who was circling/around'. Word order is slightly inverted for meter: modifiers and direction come before the head noun.
segments.s370-g75: چو is 'when' again. از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar / at a distance'. دیدش is the past verb 'saw him' (دید + enclitic object -ش). برآورد is a past tense verb meaning 'lifted/brought forth' (here 'caused to rise') and takes خشم ('anger') as its object.
segments.s370-g76: پر از ... is the construction 'full of ...' with از introducing the filling material. خاک روی literally 'dust of the face' = 'dust on (his) face'; the second clause repeats پر از with خون to yield 'full of blood'. The head noun دو چشم ('two eyes') appears at the end (inverted order for poetic effect), so altogether 'his two eyes were full of dust and full of blood.'
segments.s370-g77: به + noun phrase here marks instrumental/association: به دست اندرون = 'with (in) the inner hand' (i.e., the hand toward the body). گرز is the instrument 'mace' and follows to specify what he held. چون introduces a simile 'like', so چون سام یل = 'like Sam, the hero' in apposition.
segments.s370-g78: به پیش is a positional phrase 'before/in front of'. اندرون کشته is a noun phrase 'the slain inside/within' (اندرون qualifying کشته), and چون introduces a simile comparing them to کوه تل 'a mountain-mound' — 'the slain before him lay like a heap/mound of a mountain.' The order is inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s370-g79: نیارست is an older/poetic negative/perfective form meaning 'did not attain / could not (do)'; it negates the succeeding verbal noun. رفتنش is the verbal noun 'his going' (رفتن + enclitic -ش 'his'). بر پیش روی is a prepositional phrase 'to/in front of the face' (بر + پیش + روی); the whole clause says his attempt to go forward could not reach the front.
segments.s370-g80: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'. پنهان = 'concealment/hidden'. همی is an archaic/poetic particle meaning 'just/straightaway' or marking immediacy/continuity. تاخت is the past verb 'rushed/galloped'. بر گرد اوی is the prepositional phrase 'around him' where اوی is the third-person clitic pronoun 'him'.
segments.s370-g129: بیارید is an imperative plural (or polite) command 'bring' (classical Persian form of آوردن). گلگون is an adjective 'rosy/flower-colored' modifying the following name. لهراسپی is the proper name Lohrasp (here in adjectival/epithet form) and is the direct object of the verb.
segments.s370-g130: نهید is an imperative (from نهاد) meaning 'place/put' or 'saddle (him)'; it governs the following prepositional phrase. از برش is a short adpositional phrase with برش = 'on/upon him' (بر + pronoun ش = 'on his/him') and از used here in a spatial/idiomatic sense linked to placing something onto someone; زین گشتاسپی is a genitive phrase 'the saddle of Gūštāsp' (زین = saddle, گشتاسپی = Gūštasp). The overall instruction is 'place/put the Gūštāspian saddle on him.'
segments.s370-g131: بیاراست (perfect/simple past or causative sense here) means 'he prepared / fitted / equipped' and takes two infinitival complements. مر is the clitic pronoun 'me' (object). جستن is the infinitive 'to seek', and کینش (کین + possessive suffix) is the object 'his/that vengeance' with را marking it as definite/direct object. The clause means roughly 'he prepared/fit me to seek vengeance.'
segments.s370-g132: This is the second infinitival complement introduced by به functioning like 'for / to' + infinitive: به ورزیدن = 'to practise'. دین و آیینش is a coordinated noun phrase 'religion and its rites' (آیینش = 'its ritual/ceremony'), and را is the object marker attaching to the coordinated phrase. Together with the previous segment it yields '...to seek vengeance and to practise religion and its rites.'
segments.s370-g133: جهاندیده is an adjective 'worldly-wise' modifying the following noun. دستور here is a title/role (Dastūr / minister/counselor) or could be read as 'order' depending on context; گفتا is the archaic past form 'said' (classical narrative past). به پای is an idiomatic adverbial phrase here (roughly 'firmly' or 'resolutely') qualifying how he spoke.
segments.s370-g134: به کینه شدن is an infinitival/nominal clause 'the becoming/assuming of hatred' (به + [کینه شدن] = 'in/for my taking up hatred'). مر is the clitic 'me', and ت + را is the second-person clitic + object/topic marker ('to you' / topicalized 'you'). نیست رای literally 'there is no opinion' = 'you have no say/opinion.' Put together: 'As for my taking up hatred, you have no say.'
segments.s370-g135: به is a preposition introducing the authority/agent ('by' or 'at the command of'). فرمان دستور is a poetic doublet (two closely related nouns in apposition) meaning 'command/instruction'; دانای راز is a genitive modifier ('of the knower/wise one of the secret') that specifies whose command it is. In classical Persian the linking ezâfe is often not written, so دانای راز attaches to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s370-g136: فرود آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'he descended/alfighted' (فرود + آمد). از introduces a source or reference ('from/of'), here followed by باره, an older noun meaning 'occasion/instance' (used idiomatically here). بنشست is the past 'he sat down' formed with the verbal prefix ب-; باز is an adverb 'again' that follows the verb to mark repetition. The two verb phrases are coordinated: he descended, and then he sat down again.
segments.s370-g1: دو هفته برآمد برین کارزار is a temporal/location clause: دو هفته (two weeks) + برآمد (lit. 'arose/occurred', here idiomatically 'passed/elapsed'). برین is composed of بر (on/upon) + the enclitic ین (variant of این, 'this'), so برین کارزار = 'on this battlefield'. Word order is noun (period) + verb + locative phrase, typical of epic Persian.
segments.s370-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause ('and/when/so that'); هزمان means 'simultaneously/at the same time'. همی is the continuous/progressive particle marking ongoing action. تیره‌تر is the comparative of تیره ('more ominous/bleak'), and گشت is the past of گشتن used copulatively 'became'. کار here means 'affair/situation'. The structure: 'that at the same time the affair became darker.'
segments.s370-g3: به پیش اندر آمد = 'came forward / came into the presence': به...پیش (toward/in the presence of) + اندر آمد (entered/came in). نبرده is a negative perfect/participle of بردن ('not taken'/'not carried off') modifying زریر (the proper name); idiomatically 'Zarir, who had not been taken/captured, came forward.'
segments.s370-g4: سمندی بزرگ اندر آورده زیر = 'having brought a great steed beneath (him)': سمندی (a steed/war-horse) + بزرگ (great) ; اندر آورده is a compound perfective participle 'brought in/forward'; and زیر here means 'under/beneath' (i.e. placed the steed under/beneath him — he put it under himself or before him). The clause is participial and links to the subject who 'came forward.'
segments.s370-g5: به لشکرگه دشمن اندر فتاد = 'fell into the enemy's camp': به + لشکرگه (army-camp) + دشمن (enemy) + اندر فتاد (fell into). فتاد is the past of افتادن 'to fall' used here passive/intransitive 'fell upon/into'. Word order places the locative before the verb, a normal epic/poetic pattern.
segments.s370-g6: چو اندر گیا آتش و تیز باد = 'when into it fire and a fierce/swift wind went in': چو = 'when/as'; اندر = 'in/into'. گیا in the lemma list is given as 'plant/vegetation' which does not fit the context (here it functions as a verb 'went/entered' in the poetic text); because the supplied senses don't match, its sense index is -1. آتش = 'fire'; تیز باد = 'swift/keen wind' (تیز qualifying باد).
segments.s370-g7: همی کشت زیشان همی خوابنید is a paired clause: همی (continuous particle) + کشت (from کشتن 'killing' used as a verbal action 'kept killing') + ز ایشان ('of/from them/among them'); then همی خوابنید (they repeatedly fell asleep/fell, with خوابنید an archaic/poetic past or resultative form meaning 'fell asleep' or euphemistically 'fell dead'). The repetition of همی emphasizes ongoing, repeated action.
segments.s370-g8: مر او را نه استاد هرکش بدید is compact and idiomatic: مر (to me / as for me) + او را (him, with object marker) + نه استاد (not [regarded as] master) + هرکش بدید (no one/ whoever saw). Grammatically it expresses that 'no one saw/recognized him as a master' — negative statement with نه and the verb بدید (past of دیدن 'to see'). Word order is topical/emphatic and typical of epic diction.
segments.s370-g17: کنون is an adverb meaning “now.” اندر آمد is a verb phrase meaning “came in/entered”; classical Persian often puts the verb before the subject for poetic effect, so آمد (came) precedes the name. میانه is an adverbial/object meaning “into the middle/among,” and زریر (a proper name) follows to indicate who entered — the ezāfe linking is omitted in verse.
segments.s370-g18: چو is the comparative particle “like/as.” The two nouns form a pair of similes: گرگ دژآگاه = “a wolf that is watchful/of the fortress” and شیر دلیر = “a brave lion.” و simply links the two images. This is descriptive metaphorical language; the adjective دلیر follows and qualifies شیر.
segments.s370-g19: بکشت is an archaic past-form (here ‘he killed’); in poetry the short preverb b‑ can appear attached. او is the third-person pronoun ‘he’ placed after the verb (inverted order). همه پاک مردان من is the direct object: همه (all), پاک (noble/pure) modifies مردان (men/warriors), and من (my) follows the noun in classical word order to mark possession.
segments.s370-g20: This is a continuation of the list of the slain: سرافراز is an adjective ‘honoured/proud’ placed before گردان (troop/division). و connects this phrase to ترکان من (‘my Turks’); again من follows the noun to show possession. There is no new verb because the whole noun phrase is governed by the preceding past verb.
segments.s370-g21: یکی چاره is ‘one remedy/one course of action.’ باید is the modal ‘must/it is necessary that,’ and سگالیدنا is an older infinitival form (‑نا ending) meaning ‘to cry out / to give a cry’ here; the construction ‘چاره باید + infinitive’ expresses that a certain action is necessary.
segments.s370-g22: و گرنه is the conjunction + conditional/adversative ‘and otherwise / if not.’ ره is the object ‘the road/way,’ ترک refers to ‘Turks’ (here the people), and مالیدنا is an infinitive (archaic ‑نا) whose basic sense is ‘to rub’ but in context functions figuratively: ‘to take to / to go along’ the road. Grammatically this clause states the undesirable consequence if the remedy is not taken.
segments.s370-g23: برین‌گر is a title/epithet ‘the exalted one’ (or ‘he who ennobles’). بماند is a third-person singular present/subjunctive form ‘remain / should remain’ used here in a conditional sense. زمانی is an adverb ‘for a while/for a time,’ and چنین means ‘thus/so’; together they form a conditional temporal clause (‘if he remains thus for a while’).
segments.s370-g24: نه … نه … نه is the negative correlative ‘neither … nor … nor.’ ایتاش is a demonstrative/term here (‘these/those’ or a specific item name), ماند is the same verb ‘remain’ used negatively, and خلخ and چین are nouns listed as items that would not remain (خلخ = ‘anklet,’ چین = ‘wrinkle/fold’ or another listed item depending on textual sense). The clause enumerates things that would be gone.
segments.s370-g25: کدامست is an archaic contraction of 'کدام است' placed before the noun to ask 'which (is)'; here it introduces the question 'which man'. 'مرد' is the noun 'man/hero' (classical heroic sense). 'از شما' is a prepositional phrase 'of/among you'. 'نام خواه' is a compound: نام 'fame/reputation' + خواه (from خواستن) used attributively as 'seeking' — so 'نام‌خواه' = 'one who desires renown'. Word order follows classical Persian: question-word + noun + complement.
segments.s370-g26: که is a relative particle 'who/that' linking this clause to 'مرد'. آید پدید is a poetic inversion of پدید آید ('appear/come forth'); both verbs together indicate emergence (present/future sense: 'will come forth'). از میان سپاه is the prepositional phrase 'from the middle of the army/host'. The verb form is classical present used for an imminent/future action.
segments.s370-g27: یکی introduces an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. 'ترگ داری' literally 'rag-bearing' (ترگ 'rag' + داری 'having') functions as an attribute describing that person. خرامد is the verb 'strides/prances' (poetic present), and به پیش is an adverbial 'forward/in front'. Classical word order places the descriptive phrase before the verb.
segments.s370-g28: خنیده کند combines a participial or verbal root خنیده ('sing/sung') with the auxiliary کند ('does/makes') to express that he causes or effects singing — i.e. 'he makes his name be sung' or 'he sings'. در جهان is the adverbial 'in/throughout the world'. نام خویش is the direct object 'his own name' (خوی + ش = خویش, possessive 'own/his'), with را (object marker) understood from classical order.
segments.s370-g29: هران is a plural demonstrative 'those (of them)'. کز is the relative/prepositional particle 'who/which/from which' introducing the clause. میان باره literal 'from the middle (a) burst/volley' — باره here denotes a shower/attack (a volley). انگیزند is a plural present 'they incite/launch' (classical present form used for the action of the group).
segments.s370-g30: بگرداندش is a causative verb 'make him turn' with the object enclitic -ش 'him'. پشت here is 'back' (i.e. turn his back). و is the conjunction 'and'. بگریزند is plural present 'they flee/escape' — the same subject (those who launched the volley) performs both actions, first causing him to turn and then fleeing.
segments.s370-g31: من is the first-person subject 'I'. او را دهم: او را is the definite object 'him' + را (object marker) and دهم is the first-person present/subjunctive of 'دادن' used here with future/optative force 'I give/I will give'. دختر خویش را is the direct object 'my own daughter' — خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'own' referring back to the speaker (خوی + ش = خویش). Classical Persian allows two objects with را-marking and flexible order.
segments.s370-g32: سپارم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive of سپردن 'to entrust', here 'I will entrust'. بدو is the dative 'to him' (به + او contracted), and لشکر خویش را is the object 'my army' (لشکر 'army' + خویش 'my own' + را object marker). Word order places the verb first, then dative, then the object phrase; the possessive خویش again refers back to the speaker.
segments.s370-g97: نبرده is a past negative verb meaning 'has not been taken/has not taken' and here stands sentence-initial (verb before subject), a common poetic word order. برادرم is the noun برادر with the possessive suffix -م 'my brother' (object/subject depending on verb). فرخ زریر is a personal name (فرخ used as an epithet + زریر as the proper name). Together they form the subject following the verb.
segments.s370-g98: که introduces a subordinate clause and here functions causally ('for/because'). شیر ژیان is a compound epithet: شیر (lion) + ژیان (literally 'life' but here poetic, giving 'mighty/life-bearing lion' or 'lion of men'). آوریدی is 2nd-person past 'you brought' (آوردی with final -ی), and به زیر is a prepositional phrase 'to/under' — together 'you brought under (your control)'.
segments.s370-g99: فگندست is a past verbal form 'they threw/hurled' (poetic past). بر باره is an adverbial/accusative phrase meaning 'a shower/burst' (here of missiles); از + تاختن marks relation to the enemy's movement — 'in response to / against the charging' (از here = 'because of / at the time of'), and تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to charge'.
segments.s370-g100: بماندند is past plural 'they remained / they stayed / they halted'. گردان is the subject 'troops / squadrons'. ز (poetic form of از) here marks cause or separation 'from / because of'. انداختن is the infinitive/masdar 'to throw / to cast' (used here in the sense related to the action of charging/attacking), so the whole clause means the squadrons were left/ceased as a result of that throwing/attack.
segments.s370-g101: نیاید is a negative present/impersonal verb 'does not come' (classical form); همی is an adverbial particle giving continuous/emphatic force ('ever/still'); بانگ is the noun 'cry/clamor'. شه زادگان is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the royal-born' (شه 'royal/kingly' modifying زادگان 'born ones/offspring'). The whole phrase = 'the cry of the royal-born does not come.'
segments.s370-g102: مگر introduces an exception 'unless/except that'. کشته is a past participle 'killed/slain' and شد is the auxiliary 'be' forming a passive/result state ('was/is slain'). شاه آزادگان is the noun phrase 'the king of the free (the noble/chieftain)'. The clause reads 'unless the king of the free is slain.'
segments.s370-g103: هیونی is a plural subject 'beasts / savage creatures' (here with sense 'savage monsters'). بتازید is the past/plural verb 'they rushed / charged' (the prefix ب- appears in classical forms). تا means 'as far as / up to / until' and introduces the limit reached; رزمگاه is the place 'battlefield'.
segments.s370-g104: به نزدیکی is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the nearness/close vicinity'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' and درفش سیاه is the noun phrase 'black banner' with the adjective سیاه following the noun in normal Persian order. Together: 'to the vicinity of that black banner.'
segments.s370-g49: کدامست is an archaic interrogative copula meaning “which (one) is”; مرد is the noun ‘man/hero’; از شما is a postpositional phrase ‘of/among you’ (از + شما). چیره‌دست is an adjective ‘skillful’ modifying مرد; the whole chunk asks “Which man among you is skillful?” with the copula fronted.
segments.s370-g50: که introduces a relative clause ‘who/that’; بیرون شود is a two-word verbal phrase where بیرون (out) + شود (third-person present/subjunctive of شدن) yields ‘go/step out’ or ‘be able to go out’ in this context. پیش این پیل مست is a prepositional phrase (پیش = before/in front of) + demonstrative + noun + adjective, ‘before this intoxicated elephant.’
segments.s370-g51: هرانکو is an old indefinite relative ‘whoever.’ بدَان here is the archaic compound of the preposition ب- plus دان used to point to ‘that’ (i.e. ‘by/with that’). گردکش is a compound noun (گردک + -ش) referring to a ‘knob/neck‑handle’ or similar hold, and یازدا appears as a following modifier (in the text it functions as a name/epithet or an old-form modifier). Grammatically this unit marks the agent/condition ‘whoever by that [hold]…’.
segments.s370-g52: مر is a dative/benefactive ‘to me’ (or ‘for me’), او را is the direct object ‘him’ (را marks definiteness), ازان باره is a prepositional phrase ‘from that bāre/ burden/instance’ (ازان = from that, باره = load/occasion). بندازدا is an archaic/poetic form of the verb ‘to throw/cast’ (here used in an optative/future-like sense ‘I will cast/throw’), so the clause promises to cast/relieve him from that burden onto/for the speaker.
segments.s370-g53: چو is a conditional/temporal conjunction here meaning ‘if/when’; بخشنده‌ام is the predicate ‘I am generous’ with the first‑person suffix -ام; بیش is an adverb ‘more’ or ‘a greater share’; بسپارمش is the verb بسپار (to entrust/give) with the enclitic object -مَش = ‘him,’ giving ‘I will entrust/give him more.’
segments.s370-g54: کلاه is the noun ‘a hat/cap’; از بر چرخ is a poetic prepositional phrase literally ‘from/on the wheel’ with چرخ = ‘sky/heaven’ (i.e. ‘from heaven’); بگذارمش is the verb بگذار ‘to place/put’ with the object enclitic -مش = ‘on him,’ so the clause means ‘I will place a cap from the heavens upon him.’
segments.s370-g55: همیدون is a toponym or adverbial phrase referring to ‘that very field/arena’; نداد is the past tense negative of دادن ‘did not give’; ایچ کس literally ‘any/anyone’ here with the negative yields ‘no one’; پاسخش is پاسـخ + enclitic -ش ‘answer to him’ and functions as the direct object: ‘no one gave him an answer.’ Note the classical word order places the adverbial before the verb.
segments.s370-g56: بشد is an older/perfective form meaning ‘it became/it happened’; خیره is an adjective ‘staring/stunned’; و is the conjunction ‘and’; زرد گشت is a compound where زرد ‘pale/wan’ + گشت (became) yields ‘turned pale’; آن رخش is the demonstrative + the proper name/term for the horse (رخش, Rakhsh), so the clause says ‘that Rakhsh became stunned and turned pale.’
segments.s370-g105: ببینید is an imperative/plural or polite-form verb 'see' (from دیدن) used to call attention. کان is an archaic particle here meaning 'so/that' (classical usage, roughly 'how' or 'that so'); شاه من is the noun phrase 'my king' (شاه = king, من = 'my' / 'I' clitic marking possession). چون شدست is a classical past construction: چون = 'how/as', شدست = archaic past of شدن 'has become/was become', so the whole segment says 'See how my king has become.'
segments.s370-g106: This clause gives the result/explanation: کم = 'a little' / 'to some extent' (here an adverbial modifier), از درد او = 'from/because of his pain' (از = from; درد = physical pain/wound; او = he), دل پر از خون شدست = 'his heart became full of blood' (دل = heart; پر از = full of; خون = blood; شدست = archaic past 'became'). The phrase links causally to the previous segment (explaining his condition).
segments.s370-g107: بدین اندرون is a classical locative phrase 'in this inner place/room' (ب(ه) + این = 'to/at this' fused with اندرون = 'inner part/room'). بود is the past tense 'was', and شاه جهان is the title 'king of the world' (شاه = king; جهان = world). Together: 'the king of the world was in this inner chamber.'
segments.s370-g108: که introduces the subordinate/relative clause ('when/that'), آمد is past 'came/appeared', یکی خون = 'one drop of blood' (یکی = one/a single; خون = blood), ز دیده = 'from the eye' (ز = poetic 'from'; دیده = eye), چکان = present or verbal adjective 'dripping/letting fall' (here used with آمد to mean 'came dripping'). The clause describes a blood-drop beginning to drip from his eye.
segments.s370-g109: به شاه جهان گفت = 'he said to the king of the world' (به = to/for; شاه جهان = king of the world; گفت = said, introducing direct speech). ماه ترا is the addressee inside the reported speech: ماه is an epithet 'moon' (= 'beautiful one') used vocatively and ترا is the 2nd-person clitic 'you/your' indicating the person addressed. The segment sets up the quotation that follows.
segments.s370-g110: نگهدار is an optative/imperative-like form meaning 'preserve/keep' (classical usage can read as 'may (it) keep' or an exhortation 'guard'); تاج و سپاه = 'the crown and the army' (تاج = crown; و = and; سپاه = army/host). تـرا + را at the end (ت + را) are clitic markers: the -ت (-t) is the 2nd-person suffix ('your' / 'to you') and را is the definite/object marker/emphatic particle. Together the command/wish addresses preservation of the king's crown and army for him.
segments.s370-g111: This is a noun phrase in apposition: جهان‌پهلوان = 'world‑champion' (epithet), آن = 'that/the', زریر = the proper name 'Zarir', سوار = 'rider/horseman'. The sequence identifies the hero being spoken of: 'that world‑champion, the rider Zarir.'
segments.s370-g112: سواران ترکان = 'the horsemen of the Turks' (سواران = horsemen, ترکان = Turks in genitive relationship). بکشتند is past plural 'they killed' (the b- prefix + kشتند), and زار is an adjective/adverb meaning 'wretched' or 'in a pitiable manner' describing the victim or the manner of his state. The clause reports that the Turkish horsemen killed him (leaving him wretched).
segments.s370-g89: سوی + NP (سوی شاه چین) is a directional phrase 'toward the Chinese king' placed before the verb. برد is the past of بردن 'to bring/lead/bore' and takes اسپ 'steed/horse' as its object; the verb-object order here (برد اسپ) is typical of the older epic style. و connects this clause to what follows, and کمرش is 'his belt/girdle' (کمر + enclitic -ش = 'his'), preparing the location where the following items are placed.
segments.s370-g90: This is a noun phrase listing the objects located at his belt: درفش سیه 'the black standard/banner' (noun + following adjective) and افسر پرگهرش 'his jewelled helmet' (افسر 'helmet', پرگهر 'jeweled' with possessive -ش). Note the omission of an explicit copula—classical Persian often omits 'was/was hanging' and instead juxtaposes location (کمرش) with the items.
segments.s370-g91: سپاهش is سپاه 'army/host' + enclitic -ش 'his' = 'his army.' همه emphasizes 'all/everyone.' بانگ برداشتند is a compound where برداشتند (past, 3pl) here means 'they raised/uttered' and takes بانگ 'a cry' as its object; verb agreement is plural with the collective noun.
segments.s370-g92: همی is an archaic/poetic particle that marks the action (here a durative or emphatic sense: 'they then/utterly'); نعره 'a shout' is the object; از ابر 'from the cloud(s)' is a prepositional phrase giving source; بگذاشتند (ب‑گذاشتند) is past 3pl of گذاشتن with the prefix ب‑ and here means 'they let loose' or 'sent forth' (i.e. 'they let out a shout'). Word order places the source phrase before the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s370-g93: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' گشتاسپ is the subject. از کوه سر is an ablative/locative phrase 'from the summit of the mountain' (از = from, کوه سر = 'mountain-top' where سر = summit/top). بنگرید is a past/generic past-looking verb (ب‑ + نگری + د) meaning 'looked' or 'gazed.' The verb appears at the end as expected in classical narrative clauses.
segments.s370-g94: This clause expresses the negative perception: او را (او = 'him', را = definite object marker) is the direct object. ندید is the negative past 'did not see' (ن‑ prefix + دید). بر before ندید functions as a locative/prepositional element here ('upon/at'), and بدان رزمگه = 'on/at that battlefield' (بدان = به + آن / 'to/at that' in older usage). مر at the start is an archaic/discourse particle meaning roughly 'then' or 'thereupon' (not the personal 'me' here); it links back to the previous action and sets the clause in sequence.
segments.s370-g95: گمانی is a noun 'a conjecture/guess.' برم here is the older combination of preposition بر + enclitic م = 'to me' (i.e. 'he said to me' / 'he suggested to me'). گفت is the past verb 'said.' کان begins a simile/point of reference 'as if/that,' and گرد ماه is a nominal phrase 'round moon' (گرد used adjectivally/nominally here). Altogether this yields 'he said to me, by way of conjecture, "as if a round moon..."'.
segments.s370-g96: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that.' روشن is the adjective 'bright/clear.' بدی here is an archaic verbal form (contextually 'shone' / 'gave forth light' in older usage rather than the common noun 'evil'), and ز + و (زو, written here split) is the poetic contraction of ز او 'from him' (i.e. the light came from him). همه رزمگاه = 'the whole battlefield.' The clause as a whole says 'which shone brightly from him and lit the entire battlefield.'
segments.s370-g9: چو here is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. دانست is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'he knew/learned'. کان is an archaic/demonstrative particle meaning 'that' (introducing what was learned), and پورشاه is a proper name (the person referred to by that demonstrative). Word order is typical Classical Persian: subordinating particle + subject (ارجاسپ) + past verb + the reported content introduced by کان + name.
segments.s370-g10: سپه را places 'army' as a definite direct object (را is the object marker). همی is the older progressive/emphatic particle; the cluster کرد خواهد تباه is a periphrastic construction where کرد (here causative/do) combines with the auxiliary خواهد (future) and the adjective تباه 'ruined' to express 'would cause to be ruined / would lay waste'. The overall order is object — aspect/auxiliary cluster — result adjective.
segments.s370-g11: بدان is a prepositional phrase built from به + آن ('to that') meaning 'to that/unto that'; here it introduces the target of the utterance. لشکر خویش is a genitival/possessive phrase 'his army' (خویش + clitic ش = 'his/own'). آواز داد is a past-tense verbal phrase where داد is 'gave' and together means 'gave a cry' or more generally 'called'/'issued an order'.
segments.s370-g12: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'saying that'). چونین is an adverb 'thus/like this'. همی precedes the verbal complex as a progressive/emphatic particle. داد خواهید داد contains the verb داد 'give' with the future auxiliary خواهید (2nd-person plural) — literally 'you will give' — so the whole clause is a quoted instruction: 'you will give [the cry] thus.'
segments.s370-g13: دو هفته is a numeral + noun 'two weeks'. برآمد is a past verb meaning 'came/passed/occurred' (here 'passed'). بر این (orthographically shown as بر + ین) is the locative/relational phrase 'upon/there' (بر + این = 'on/at this'), and بر درنگ ('بر' + درنگ) is an idiomatic expression meaning 'with regard to delay' — here best read as 'without delay' or 'there was no delay' in the narrative. Word order places the temporal span first, then the verb and locational/qualifying phrase.
segments.s370-g14: نبینم is negative + present/subjunctive 1st-person singular 'I do not see / I shall not see' (the -م ending marks 1st singular). همی again gives a progressive/continuous nuance. روی فرجام جنگ is the object phrase: روی 'face/appearance' used with فرجام جنگ 'the end/outcome of the war' — together 'the face/appearance of the outcome' meaning idiomatically 'the outcome'. So the clause means 'I do not (yet) see the outcome of the battle.'
segments.s370-g15: بکردند is a past 3rd-person plural verb 'they made/did' (here 'they formed/raised'). گردان is the direct object 'a troop/unit'. گشتاسپ شاه follows as the beneficiary/owner 'Gushtasp the king' (name + title). Classical Persian allows this order where object + verb + beneficiary/title appear in this sequence.
segments.s370-g16: بسی is an archaic quantitative/adverbial word meaning 'many/much' (sense not listed in the provided senses for this token, so mapped to -1). نامداران لشکر is a genitival phrase 'the renowned/illustrious ones of the army' (نامداران = famed men). تباه is a predicative adjective 'ruined/destroyed' — here indicating that many of the army's famed men were destroyed or the army was laid waste.
segments.s370-g65: شوم is an optative/subjunctive first‑person form ('may I be' / 'let me be'); here it introduces a wish or daring act. پیش + آن پیل = 'in front of that elephant' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of'); آشفته مست are two adjectival modifiers placed after پیل (typical in poetic/older Persian), meaning 'disturbed, intoxicated' and together qualify the elephant.
segments.s370-g66: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic modal particle ('so/thus/if only' / 'somehow') that softens or colors the condition. یابم is first‑person subjunctive/present ('I might find / I could get'); بران پیل دست is the object phrase where بران ('on/upon that') + پیل ('that elephant') combine with دست ('hand' — here physical 'hand' / trunk) meaning 'to lay hands on that elephant'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s370-g67: به خاک افگنم is a verbal phrase meaning 'I will throw/cast into the dust' (افگنم here is an archaic/poetic verbal form). تنش is the direct object 'his body' (تن + enclitic ـش = 'his body'). ای شهریار is a vocative 'O king' inserted after the object.
segments.s370-g68: مگر introduces an exception 'unless'. بر دهد is a compressed poetic ordering where بر (on/upon) precedes the verb دهد ('gives' / 'brings about'); گردش روزگار is the agent/subject 'the turning/rotation of the times' (i.e., Fate or the wheel of fortune). Together the clause means 'unless the turn of Fortune gives/changes things.'
segments.s370-g69: ازو شاد شد شاه is a typical Persian ordering: ازو ('because of him' / 'at him') + شاد شد ('became glad') + شاه ('the king') — 'the king became glad because of him'. و کرد آفرین is a terse phrase 'and (he) made/uttered "آفرین"' (i.e., he praised him / cried 'bravo').
segments.s370-g70: بدادش here functions as a past action 'he gave to him' (verb داد with the prefix/poetic form ب‑ and enclitic ـش 'to him'); بدو is the dative 'to him'. باره خویش is the item given (باره = an article/gear/load; خویش + ـش = 'his own') and زین is 'saddle'. Word order places the recipient before the goods (بدو باره خویش و زین = 'he gave to him his bāreh and saddle').
segments.s370-g71: بدو is the clitic prepositional phrase 'to him' (to + 3rd-person object) placed before the verb; داد is the past tense verb 'gave'. ژوپین is the direct object 'gambeson / padded jacket' and زهرابدار is an adjective phrase 'poison-bearing / poisonous' placed after the noun, a common adjective order in classical Persian.
segments.s370-g72: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'ژوپین' ('which...'). از + آهنین کوه = 'from the iron mountain' (از = from; آهنین = iron/iron-made; کوه = mountain). کردی here functions as an adjectival modifier 'Kurdish / of the Kurds' qualifying کوه. گذار is a noun here ('passage/thing made / the article produced') and together the clause reads 'which was (made) from the iron of the Kurdish mountain.'
segments.s370-g137: به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'to the army' with the preposition به before the addressee. بگفتا is an archaic past tense third-person singular 'he said' (the prefixed b- and final -a are poetic/classical forms). کدامست شیر is a copular interrogative: کدامست 'which is' (interrogative + copula) followed by the noun شیر 'lion' as the predicate; the whole clause is the content of what he said.
segments.s370-g138: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking this clause to شیر. باز is an adverb 'again/back' placed before the verb, and آورد (آورد) is the verb 'brings/causes to come' (classical past-form spelling). کین is the object 'vengeance/hatred' and فرخ زریر is a personal name in genitive relationship to کین ('Farrokh-Zarir's vengeance').
segments.s370-g139: که introduces a relative clause. پیش is an adverb 'before/previously' modifying the verb افگند 'threw/cast' (past tense). باره here means 'burden/load' (object) and بر + کین marks the target 'upon (his) vengeance/hatred'; اوی is the third-person enclitic 'his' referring back to the antecedent.
segments.s370-g140: که again introduces the second relative clause. باز is 'again' placed before the verb آورد 'brought/returned'. باره and زین form a coordinated object 'the burden and the saddle', connected by و, and اوی (his) is the possessive enclitic applying to (at least) زین and likely both nouns in context.
segments.s370-g141: پذیرفتن is the infinitive/masdar 'to accept' (here nominalized: 'being accepted' or 'to be received'). اندر is the classical locative/prepositional 'in/within/by' and خدای جهان is a genitive phrase 'the Lord/God of the world' (خدای governing جهان). Together they mark being accepted/received in the presence or domain of God.
segments.s370-g142: A parallel infinitive/predicate: پذیرفتن 'to accept/receive' with a coordinated object راستان و مهان — راستان 'the righteous/upright ones' used as a noun and مهان 'guests/visitors'. The verb is repeated for rhetorical parallelism; no overt object marker appears before راستان here.
segments.s370-g143: که introduces the clause. هر کز is a correlative indefinite 'whoever/whosoever' (هر = every, کز = who/that). میانه is a noun 'middle/center' used adverbially here ('places himself in the middle' / 'places himself between/among'), and نهد (نَهَد) is the classical present/stative verb 'sets/places' (3rd person). پیش پای is a locative phrase 'before/in front of the foot' with پیش preceding the noun پای.
segments.s370-g144: This clause gives the promise/award. مر is an archaic dative/benefactive pronoun (classical form 'to/for me' or acting to mark the recipient phrase in this older syntax) used with او را to indicate the person who will receive the gift; دهم is the first-person singular 'I give' (understood here as 'I will give'). دخترم را is the direct object 'my daughter' (را marks the definite object) and همای is the proper name in apposition (the daughter's name). Word order is classical: recipient pronouns and particles can appear before the verb, and the object marked by را follows the verb.

sentences.s370-s60: Gushtasp kept saying, 'O king, they have slain the light of your heart — alas!'
sentences.s370-s17: They gave no answer back to his army; the proud army became badly frightened.

sentences.s370-s19: He kept striking them on every side beneath the feet of Iran's commander of auspicious counsel.
sentences.s370-s20: When Arjasp saw (this) he was so astonished that his bright day became a dark night.

sentences.s370-s31: He said to Arjasp, 'O lofty sun, in strength and in body like Afrasiab!'
sentences.s370-s32: I have brought this my life before you; I made this sweet life into a shield before you.
sentences.s370-s61: After that the knower said to Jamasp, “What shall I say now to King Lohrasp?”
sentences.s370-s62: How shall I send an envoy to Badr? What shall I say to that aged father?
sentences.s370-s63: What shall I say? What have I done, my beloved, that a rider took you — one who had not taken you before?
sentences.s370-s64: “Alas!” cried that prince; “Alas, when the radiant moon went within and was struck by the blade!”
sentences.s370-s41: He threw off the poison‑laden gambeson, and from hiding charged at that princely rider.
sentences.s370-s42: The king’s cuirass was cast aside; his kingly body became drenched in blood.
sentences.s370-s43: Then, from that encounter the king fell — alas for that fine princely rider.
sentences.s370-s44: That bannerless, vile one alighted and cleanly drew out all his weapons.
sentences.s370-s21: He said again, "O my great ones — my Tegins, my chosen host!"
sentences.s370-s22: Look, kinsmen and attendants; behold the lamentations of the weary.
sentences.s370-s23: From that wound in that flank comes a fire which is Sam's mace and Arash's arrow.
sentences.s370-s24: Which you said would burn my army — now indeed it will set my realm ablaze.
sentences.s370-s37: When the ugly, shameless sorcery turned toward that wise rider who was circling,
sentences.s370-s38: When he saw him from afar, anger rose up; his two eyes were full of dust and full of blood.
sentences.s370-s39: With a mace in his inner hand, like the hero Sam he struck; before him the slain lay piled like a mountain-mound.
sentences.s370-s40: He could not reach to go before his face, so from concealment he straightaway rushed around him.
sentences.s370-s65: Bring the rosy Lohrasp, and put Gūštāsp's saddle on him.
sentences.s370-s66: He made me ready to seek vengeance, and to practise religion and its rites.
sentences.s370-s67: The worldly-wise Dastur said firmly, 'You have no say in my taking up hatred.'
sentences.s370-s68: By the command of the knower of secrets he descended, and again sat down.
sentences.s370-s1: Two weeks passed on that battlefield, and meanwhile the affair grew ever darker.
sentences.s370-s2: Zarir, who had not been taken, came forward and brought a great steed beneath him.
sentences.s370-s3: It fell into the enemy camp; when fire and a swift wind entered it.
sentences.s370-s4: It kept slaughtering them and they fell (asleep/dead); no one saw him as their master.
sentences.s370-s9: Now Zarrir came into the midst, like a wolf watchful of the fortress and like a brave lion.
sentences.s370-s10: He killed all my noble men — my proud division and my Turks.
sentences.s370-s11: One remedy must be to cry out; otherwise the Turks will take to the road.
sentences.s370-s12: If that exalted one were to remain thus for a while, none of these would remain — neither ītāš, nor anklet, nor chin.
sentences.s370-s13: Which man among you, seeking renown, will come forth from the midst of the host?
sentences.s370-s14: One, clad in a rag, strides proudly forward and makes his name be sung in the world.
sentences.s370-s15: Those who launch a volley from the midst will make him turn his back and will flee.
sentences.s370-s16: I will give him my daughter and will entrust my army to him.
sentences.s370-s49: My brother Farrokh Zarrir has not been taken, for you brought the lion of life under (your control).
sentences.s370-s50: They hurled a shower (of missiles) at the charge; the squadrons ceased (stopped) from charging.
sentences.s370-s51: The cry of the royal-born does not come forth, unless the king of the free is slain.
sentences.s370-s52: Savage beasts rushed to the battlefield, up to the vicinity of that black banner.
sentences.s370-s25: Which one of you is a skillful man who will step out before this intoxicated elephant?
sentences.s370-s26: Whoever (does so) with that neck‑knob (or hold), I will throw him off that burden for me.
sentences.s370-s27: If I am generous, I will give him more; I will place a cap from the heavens upon him.
sentences.s370-s28: No one in that very arena gave him any answer; his Rakhsh became stunned and turned pale.
sentences.s370-s53: See how my king has become: because of his pain his heart is full of blood.
sentences.s370-s54: The world-king was in this inner chamber, when a drop of blood came dripping from his eye.
sentences.s370-s55: He said to the king of the world: 'O moon (you), keep/guard — your crown and your army.'
sentences.s370-s56: That world‑champion, the rider Zarir — the Turks' horsemen killed him, wretchedly.
sentences.s370-s45: He turned his steed toward the Chinese king, and at his belt were the black standard and his jewelled helmet.
sentences.s370-s46: His army all raised a cry; they let out a shout from the clouds.
sentences.s370-s47: When Gushtasp looked down from the mountain-top, he did not see him on that battlefield.
sentences.s370-s48: He offered this conjecture to me: ‘That round moon — which shone brightly from him — lit up the whole battlefield.’
sentences.s370-s5: When Arjasp learned that Pourshah would lay waste to the army.
sentences.s370-s6: He called to that army of his, saying, 'You shall give the cry like this.'
sentences.s370-s7: Two weeks passed there without delay; I did not yet see the outcome of the battle.
sentences.s370-s8: They formed a troop for King Gushtasp; many renowned men of the army were ruined.
sentences.s370-s33: May I stand before that bewildered, drunken elephant — if only I could get hold of that elephant's hand (trunk).
sentences.s370-s34: I will cast his body to the dust, O king, unless the turning of Fate intervenes.

sentences.s370-s36: He gave him a poison-bearing gambeson that had been made from the iron of the Kurdish mountain.
